stories.body: U.S. Central Command said American forces downed four Iranian drones aimed at maritime traffic near the Strait of Hormuz and then hit coastal radar and surveillance sites on Goruk and Qeshm Island, intensifying a war that began with U.S. and Israeli strikes in February; President Donald Trump told NBC the campaign has reduced Iran’s missile arsenal to about 22% of prewar levels even as Tehran links any cease-fire to easing sanctions and reopening its ports, while reporting from Bandar Abbas shows fishermen only recently returning to waters still dotted with IRGC-seized cargo vessels under a fragile truce that has yet to fully restore shipping through the strait.
stories.headline: U.S. strikes Iranian radar sites after drone attacks escalate Strait tensions

stories.body: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi told Lebanon’s Al Mayadeen that any interim peace agreement with Washington must include a cease-fire between Israel and Hezbollah and a withdrawal of Israeli forces from southern Lebanon, underscoring Tehran’s insistence on linking the U.S.-Iran war to the parallel conflict; senior Iranian officials reaffirmed support for Hezbollah, warned Israel against strikes on Beirut, and noted that U.S. forces continue boarding tankers while Iran fires warning shots in the Gulf of Oman, even as humanitarian agencies say the broader confrontation is pushing millions toward hunger.
stories.headline: Iran ties regional peace deal to Israeli withdrawal as it doubles down on Hezbollah

stories.body: Reuters reports Iran is taking a “stern” approach to a proposed interim deal that would pause its war with the United States and reopen the largely shuttered Strait of Hormuz, with Tehran seeking sanctions waivers, access to oil revenues, and leverage over the chokepoint while Trump says talks could extend the April cease-fire within a week despite continued tit-for-tat strikes, Israeli operations in Lebanon, and warnings from energy officials that global inventories could hit historic lows as shipping disruptions fuel price spikes.
stories.headline: Tehran weighs proposed U.S. agreement to halt war as stalemate persists

stories.body: An Amnesty International investigation based on 247 interviews, multimedia evidence and satellite imagery concludes Sudan’s Rapid Support Forces conducted a systematic campaign of murder, torture, rape, enslavement and ethnic cleansing against non-Arab civilians during the 2024-2025 siege and capture of El Fasher, leaving tens of thousands dead, orphaning children, destroying towns such as Abu Zerega, and implicating commanders Gedo Hamdan Ahmed Mohamed, Abbas Khater Bakhit and Al-Fateh Abdullah Idris in abuses that the group says demand an immediate cease-fire and international protection force.
stories.headline: Amnesty accuses Sudan’s RSF of crimes against humanity in El Fasher offensive

stories.body: According to data shared with the Associated Press, Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrested about 10,000 people in a five-day span at the end of June after shifting from high-profile sweeps to quieter tactics meant to meet President Donald Trump’s mass-deportation goals, underscoring the administration’s intensifying enforcement push even as protests continue outside federal facilities and the White House touts new funding for immigration operations.
stories.headline: ICE says 10,000 migrants arrested in five days amid Trump deportation surge

stories.body: The Washington Post reports that after 118 people died in Philadelphia’s 1993 heat wave, the city built a heat-health warning system, community check-ins, and cooling centers that climate scientists say still save lives today, offering a template for U.S. cities facing longer, deadlier hot spells driven by climate change and currently bracing for multi-day heat emergencies.
stories.headline: Philadelphia’s 1993 heat-wave reforms now guide national extreme-heat planning

stories.body: Politico describes FEMA’s Washington operations floor where Homeland Security, FBI, State Department and other agencies hold daily “WISLE” calls during the World Cup to manage heat advisories, security risks, visa issues, cartel threats and counter-drone missions, with officials highlighting confiscation of more than 500 drones, coordination with foreign police hubs, and parallel federal efforts to combat trafficking and fentanyl while keeping stadium operations “green.”
stories.headline: Inside DHS World Cup command center coordinating heat, security and global threats

stories.body: New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill signed a law charging companies with at least 50 employees on Medicaid between $325 and $725 per beneficiary annually, raising an expected $145 million this year to offset higher state Medicaid costs as federal rules shift, while California lawmakers ordered options for a similar fee, other states considered related measures, and business groups warn the policy penalizes firms even as supporters cite fairness and looming coverage gaps.
stories.headline: New Jersey to bill employers whose workers rely on Medicaid, foreshadowing other states

stories.body: Judge Santiago Pedraz of Spain’s Audiencia Nacional has placed Guardia Civil Director Mercedes González and operations chief Manuel Llamas under investigation for alleged perversion of justice and obstruction tied to the “Leire Díez case,” in which prosecutors say internal disciplinary inquiries were weaponized to intimidate UCO anti-corruption agents probing matters affecting the PSOE, the government and Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez’s family, with both officials due in court on July 16 and disciplinary actions accused of creating a chilling effect inside the elite unit.
stories.headline: Guardia Civil director and deputy charged with obstructing corruption probes

stories.body: Government sources told El País that Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez and Interior Minister Fernando Grande-Marlaska maintain full confidence in Guardia Civil Director Mercedes González, arguing her meeting with former Socialist operative Leire Díez did not involve pressure on investigators and that her court summons is merely to answer questions, signaling the executive’s refusal to seek her resignation amid accusations of obstructing anti-corruption work.
stories.headline: Sánchez backs embattled Guardia Civil leadership despite judicial probe

stories.body: Juan Manuel Moreno secured reelection as Andalusia’s premier with 68 votes after granting Vox a single vice-presidential portfolio covering deregulation, tourism, justice and local administration, a limited but symbolically significant coalition that imports Vox’s “national priority” planks while letting the PP retain most of the €51.6 billion budget and framing the concessions as pragmatic to avoid legislative deadlock.
stories.headline: PP seals governing pact with Vox in Andalusia, embracing “national priority” agenda

stories.body: El País reports that although 2.4 million descendants of exiled or emigrant Spaniards have booked consular appointments under the “ley de nietos,” most cannot even file paperwork before the 2027 general election deadline and the bulk of cases will not be resolved until well into the next decade, undercutting PP and Vox allegations that the government is engineering massive electoral fraud through expedited citizenship grants.
stories.headline: Data debunks fraud claims over Spain’s ‘law of grandchildren’ naturalizations

stories.body: Spain’s Interior Ministry says 1,174,968 people applied before the June 30 deadline for the government’s extraordinary migrant regularization, with 11,000 already granted status and about 610,000 cases under review; El País details the demographic breakdown as authorities brace for a lengthy processing campaign to integrate applicants by nationality, age and gender.
stories.headline: Spain’s extraordinary regularization drive draws 1.17 million applications

stories.body: Betevé and 3Cat report that a 15-year-old boy died late Thursday after rivals fired at least two shots near Parc de la Pegaso in Barcelona’s La Sagrera neighborhood, prompting the Mossos d’Esquadra to launch a homicide investigation under secrecy orders, deploy a search for suspects believed to be Latin gang members armed with a machete, and warn that the killing marks the city’s fifth gun-death of 2026 amid a broader rise in gang shootouts linked to drug trafficking.
stories.headline: Teenager killed in gang-related shooting at Parc de la Pegaso

stories.body: El Periódico says Barcelona expects up to 1.3 million spectators as the 2026 Tour de France opens with a 19.6-kilometer team time trial this weekend, triggering 100 street closures, 40,000 protective barriers, extensive bus diversions, reinforced metro service and similar restrictions in other Catalan cities along the route, all compounded by peak summer tourism and vacation traffic.
stories.headline: Barcelona braces for Tour de France kickoff with sweeping closures and security

stories.body: 3Cat reports a cable failure and small fire at the Collblanc electrical substation knocked out power Monday for up to 16,781 customers in Barcelona, L’Hospitalet, Esplugues and Cornellà, leaving many without air conditioning until technicians restored service around 5 a.m.; authorities say a repair attempt briefly worsened the blackout even as residents took to the streets to escape the heat.
stories.headline: Power outage affects 17,000 customers across Barcelona’s metro area

stories.body: According to 3Cat, a driver suspected of reckless, wrong-way driving through Poblenou fled Mossos d’Esquadra officers Sunday morning and slammed into another car at the intersection of Carrer Pallars and Carrer Fluvià, killing both drivers, critically injuring a passenger who was taken to Hospital del Mar, and prompting officials to note that the suspect likely drove under the influence as Barcelona’s 2026 traffic death toll rose to eight.
stories.headline: Two dead after driver fleeing Mossos collides with another car in Barcelona
